The way Joseph was freed from prison involved Pharaoh's dreams, as described here. Now that God no longer speaks to us in that way, it does not matter how little we either pay attention to dreams or tell them. Telling foolish dreams can only result in foolish talk. But these dreams showed they were sent by God; when he awoke, Pharaoh’s spirit was troubled.

God's timing for the enlargement of his people is the most suitable time. If the chief butler had arranged for Joseph to be released from prison, it is likely he would have returned to the land of the Hebrews. Then he would not have been so blessed himself, nor such a blessing to his family, as he later proved. When introduced to Pharaoh, Joseph gives honor to God. Pharaoh dreamed that he stood on the bank of the river Nile and saw the cows, both the fat ones and the lean ones, come out of the river. Egypt has no rain, but the abundance of the year depends upon the overflowing of the river Nile. See how many ways Providence provides its gifts; yet our dependence remains the same upon the First Cause, who makes every creature what it is to us, whether it be rain or river. Observe to what changes the comforts of this life are subject. We cannot be sure that tomorrow will be as today, or next year as this. We must learn how to lack, as well as how to have plenty. Note God’s goodness in sending seven years of abundance before those of famine, so provision could be made. The produce of the earth is sometimes more, and sometimes less; yet, on average, he who gathers much has nothing over, and he who gathers little has no lack. And see the fleeting nature of our worldly possessions. The great harvests of the years of abundance were completely lost and consumed during the years of famine; and what seemed to be so much barely served to keep people alive. There is bread that lasts to eternal life, which it is worth striving for. Those who make the things of this world their good things will find little pleasure in remembering that they received them.

Joseph gave good advice to Pharaoh. Fair warning should always be followed by sound counsel. God has in his word told us of a day of trial before us, when we will need all the grace we can have. Therefore, prepare accordingly now. Pharaoh gave Joseph an honorable testimony: he is a man in whom the spirit of God is; and such men ought to be valued. Pharaoh bestowed marks of honor upon Joseph. He gave him a name that expressed the value he had for him, Zaphnath-paaneah, “a revealer of secrets.” This promotion of Joseph encourages all to trust in God. Some translate Joseph’s new name, “the savior of the world.” The brightest glories, even of the upper world, are placed upon Christ, the highest trust is lodged in his hand, and all power is given him, both in heaven and earth.

In the names of his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim, Joseph acknowledged God’s providence. 1. He was made to forget his misery. 2. He was made fruitful in the land of his affliction. The seven years of abundance came and ended. We ought to look forward to the end of the days, both of our prosperity and of our opportunity. We must not be secure in prosperity, nor lazy in making good use of opportunity. Years of plenty will end; whatever your hand finds to do, do it; and gather during gathering time. The famine came, and the famine was not only in Egypt, but in other lands. Joseph was diligent in laying up supplies while abundance lasted. He was prudent and careful in distributing them when the famine came. Joseph was engaged in useful and important labors. Yet it was during this activity that his father Jacob said, “Joseph is no more!” What a large portion of our troubles would be resolved if we knew the whole truth! Let these events lead us to Jesus. There is a famine of the bread of life throughout the whole earth. Go to Jesus, and do what he commands you. Attend to his voice, apply to him; he will open his treasures and satisfy with goodness the hungry soul of every age and nation, without money and without price. But those who disregard this provision must starve, and his enemies will be destroyed.
